title: Open Road
description: A minor, 132 BPM, Am to F to C, 4-on-floor drums, and a lead melody that opens out over a longer arrangement. The writing is fully hand-held, but the final song is shaped like something you could upload and get useful critique on.
what_youll_learn: How Am to F to C creates forward motion while staying beginner-friendly
How to voice a lead so every downbeat lands on a clear chord tone
How to use a 4-bar harmonic loop and still make a full song
How to stage a three-chord track so the second drop feels release-ready instead of repeated

steps.title: Concept and setup
steps.instruction: Create a new Ableton Live set at 132 BPM and save it as 'original-06-open-road'. Make these tracks: DRUMS, BASS, PAD, LEAD, and a return track called VERB. The brief is simple: warm A minor verses, a brighter F and C lift, and a lead that feels like it is traveling ahead of the chords.
steps.why: This track adds one extra harmonic stop compared with Night Walk, so the arrangement feels more narrative without getting technically difficult.
steps.tip: Keep thinking in 4-bar chunks. This entire track is built from one 4-bar idea.

steps.title: Load the drum rack
steps.instruction: On DRUMS load a Drum Rack and place these exact samples: C1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 1/KICKS/PH UKG1 ONESHOT Cash_Kick.wav', D1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 2/One Shots/Clap/PH_UKG2_Clap_10.wav', F#1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 2/One Shots/Closed Hat/PH_UKG2_ClosedHat_6.wav', G#1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 2/One Shots/Shaker/PH_UKG2_Shaker_3.wav'.
steps.why: Cash_Kick has a faster front edge than Hackney_Kick, which helps the track feel tighter at 132 BPM.
steps.tip: Using all four drum sounds from the Production Hut garage folders keeps the kit cohesive even though the folders come from two different volumes.

steps.title: Program the drum pattern
steps.instruction: Create a 2-bar DRUMS clip. All note lengths are 0.25 beats. KICK C1: 1.1.1 vel 96, 1.2.1 vel 88, 1.3.1 vel 94, 1.4.1 vel 86, 2.1.1 vel 96, 2.2.1 vel 89, 2.3.1 vel 95, 2.4.1 vel 87. CLAP D1: 1.2.1 vel 91, 1.4.1 vel 89, 2.2.1 vel 91, 2.4.1 vel 89. CLOSED HAT F#1: 1.1.3 vel 63, 1.2.3 vel 61, 1.3.3 vel 64, 1.4.3 vel 59, 2.1.3 vel 63, 2.2.3 vel 61, 2.3.3 vel 65, 2.4.3 vel 58. SHAKER G#1: 1.2.3 vel 46, 1.4.3 vel 43, 2.2.3 vel 46, 2.4.3 vel 43. Group DRUMS and add Glue Compressor with Ratio 4:1, Attack 3 ms, Release Auto, Threshold -5 dB, Soft Clip On.
steps.why: The pattern is familiar enough to feel stable, but the shaker on the offbeats gives it forward pull so the lead has something to ride on top of.
steps.tip: If the hats feel too sharp, lower only the hat track velocity values by 4, not the whole DRUMS group.

steps.title: Write the bass line
steps.instruction: On BASS load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Bass -  Sinesation'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Create a 4-bar MIDI clip and draw these exact notes: A1 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 92; E2 at 1.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74; F1 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 89; C2 at 2.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; C2 at 3.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 90; G1 at 3.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 70; C1 at 4.1.1, length 3.0 beats, velocity 86; E1 at 4.4.1, length 0.5 beats, velocity 68.
steps.why: The long roots make the harmony clear, and the small fifth pickups stop the low end from feeling static.
steps.tip: Bars 3 and 4 both belong to C. That extra time on the final chord is what makes the loop feel settled before returning to A minor.

steps.title: Process the bass
steps.instruction: After Serum add Saturator, Compressor, EQ Eight, and Utility. Saturator: Drive 4.8 dB, Soft Clip On, Output -2.8 dB. Compressor: Sidechain On, Audio From DRUMS, Threshold -19 dB, Ratio 4:1, Attack 0.30 ms, Release 92 ms. EQ Eight: High-Pass 30 Hz at 24 dB slope. Utility: Width 0 percent, Gain 0 dB. Set the BASS fader to -5.0 dB.
steps.why: The processing is nearly the same as track 05 because the job is the same: keep the sub audible, controlled, and out of the kick's way.
steps.tip: If the low end feels too thick in bar 3, that is usually the long C note touching the kick. Trust the sidechain first before changing the MIDI.

steps.title: Load the pad and lock in the chords
steps.instruction: On PAD load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Pad- Apricots'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Your three chords are A minor = A, C, E; F major = F, A, C; and C major = C, E, G.
steps.why: Am and F share A and C, and Am and C share C and E. Those shared notes are why this progression feels connected instead of jumpy.
steps.tip: This progression sounds familiar because it uses lots of shared tones. That is not a weakness. It is what makes it beginner-friendly.

steps.title: Program the pad and return reverb
steps.instruction: Create a 4-bar PAD clip. Draw A3 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; C4 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; E4 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; F3 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; A3 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; C4 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; C3 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 72; E3 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 68; G3 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 64. On VERB add Reverb with Decay Time 2.5 s, Pre-Delay 10 ms, Dry/Wet 100 percent, then EQ Eight High-Pass 220 Hz at 24 dB slope. On PAD add Compressor with Sidechain On, Audio From DRUMS, Threshold -22 dB, Ratio 4:1, Attack 0.10 ms, Release 130 ms. Set PAD send to VERB at -18.0 dB and PAD fader to -11.5 dB.
steps.why: The last chord gets two full bars so the listener has time to hear the phrase resolve before it loops.
steps.tip: Keep the pad soft. The lead will do the obvious melodic talking in this track.

steps.title: Write the lead melody
steps.instruction: On LEAD load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Lead- Just'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Create a 4-bar clip and draw these exact notes: A4 at 1.1.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 82; C5 at 1.3.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 76; E5 at 1.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; F4 at 2.1.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 80; A4 at 2.2.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74; C5 at 2.3.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 70; G4 at 3.1.1, length 1.5 beats, velocity 79; E4 at 3.3.1, length 0.5 beats, velocity 73; C4 at 3.4.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 76; G4 at 4.1.1, length 1.0 beats, velocity 78; E4 at 4.2.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; C4 at 4.4.1, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74. After Serum add Simple Delay with Time Left 1/8, Time Right 1/8, Feedback 14 percent, Dry/Wet 10 percent. Set LEAD send to VERB at -20.0 dB and the LEAD fader to -9.5 dB.
steps.why: Every bar opens on a chord tone that clearly belongs to the chord underneath it, so the melody sounds confident instead of accidental. The short delay gives the lead a little travel without pulling focus off the groove.
steps.tip: The delay should widen the lead, not make it wash out. If you hear a separate echo, lower Dry/Wet first.

steps.title: Create vocal space
steps.instruction: On PAD add EQ Eight after the Compressor: High-Pass 135 Hz, Bell 900 Hz at -3.5 dB with Q 1.60, Bell 2.2 kHz at -2.5 dB with Q 1.30. On LEAD add EQ Eight: High-Pass 220 Hz, Bell 3.0 kHz at -2.0 dB with Q 1.20. Leave the reverb return untouched.
steps.why: These cuts stop the pad and lead from filling the same speech-focused frequencies a singer would use later.
steps.tip: When the mix is right, the lead should feel in front of the pad even though the pad is louder in total frequency area.

steps.title: Arrange the sections
steps.instruction: Build the track exactly like this. Bars 1-8: intro, DRUMS only. Bars 9-16: drop 1A, DRUMS plus BASS plus PAD. Bars 17-24: drop 1B, add LEAD. Bars 25-28: break A, BASS only. Bars 29-32: break B, PAD only. Delete all clips at bar 32 beat 4 for a 1-beat silence. Bars 33-48: drop 2A, all four tracks. Bars 49-64: drop 2B, keep all four tracks playing, automate the LEAD track fader from -9.5 dB at bar 57 to -8.5 dB at bar 64, and automate the PAD send to VERB from -18 dB at bar 49 to -15 dB at bar 64. Bars 65-72: outro, mute LEAD at bar 65, mute PAD at bar 69, mute BASS at bar 71, and leave DRUMS until the end of bar 72.
steps.why: This makes the harmony easier to hear in the break, then gives the second half a proper lift through exact staging and automation rather than extra writing.
steps.tip: Bars 57-64 should feel like the peak because the lead opens slightly more, not because the track suddenly gets crowded.

steps.title: Set the mix
steps.instruction: Set DRUMS group to -4.0 dB, BASS to -5.0 dB, PAD to -11.5 dB, LEAD to -9.5 dB, VERB return to -18.5 dB, and Master to 0.0 dB. Add Limiter on Master with Ceiling -0.30 dB and Lookahead 1 ms.
steps.why: The lead needs to be heard clearly, but it should still sound attached to the groove rather than sitting like a separate pop vocal.
steps.tip: If the lead feels too wide, leave it alone. This lesson is about note placement, not stereo decoration.

steps.title: Export Open Road
steps.instruction: Export bars 1 through 72 as WAV, 24-bit, 44.1 kHz with filename 'original-06-open-road-v1.wav'. Listen to bars 49-64 after export and confirm the progression is still obvious and the later section feels more open than bars 17-24 without sounding detached from the groove.
steps.why: A release-ready garage sketch still has to communicate harmony clearly when the arrangement changes. This export check makes sure the bigger ending is musical, not just louder.
steps.tip: If bars 25-32 feel empty instead of intentional, lower the VERB return less in the break next time, not the bass louder.
